topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee was cold in my hands, a bitter reminder of the sudden, heavy drop in my stomach. Liam had just casually mentioned it over lunch—a plane ticket, a new apartment in Seattle, a whole life away. I stared at the chipped ceramic mug, tracing the rim with my thumb until the porcelain felt rough against my skin. My breath hitched, a shallow, quick thing in my chest. I tried to focus on the mundane sounds of the cafe, the clatter of plates, but everything seemed muted, distant. When he finally looked up, his smile was bright, uncomplicated, and it felt like a spotlight shining on a fissure in my own composure. "It’s exciting, right?" he asked, completely oblivious to the sudden tightening around my throat. I managed a tight nod, the sound barely escaping my lips, and I quickly grabbed my bag, needing the solid weight of something tangible to anchor me against the sudden, unsettling drift.

[story 2]

I kept replaying the conversation in my head, the words echoing hollowly. It wasn't just a change of address; it was an amputation. Every familiar landmark—the route we used to walk home, the specific worn spot on the couch where we always watched bad movies—suddenly felt tainted, like a photograph that had been subtly altered. My hands started shaking, a fine tremor that I tried to mask by gripping the edge of the table until my knuckles were white. I looked down at my reflection in the darkened window; my eyes looked too wide, too wet. When Liam finally spoke about the logistics, his voice was cheerful, but I heard only static. I managed a weak, "Oh. Wow," and the word tasted like ash. I excused myself abruptly, the movement jerky, and walked out into the noisy street, needing the sheer volume of the outside world to drown out the sudden, profound quiet inside my own skull.

[story 3]

The silence in my apartment was oppressive, thick enough to chew on. I paced the length of the living room three times, my footsteps echoing too loudly on the hardwood floor. I kept running the scenario over and over: Liam, packing boxes, the unfamiliar city skyline, the distance growing between us until it became an uncrossable chasm. A coldness seeped into my bones, a deep, internal chill that had nothing to do with the temperature. I stopped by the window, pulling the curtain aside just enough to see the street below, feeling intensely separate from the world rushing by. I didn't want to confront him; the thought of hearing the details, of seeing the genuine excitement on his face about this massive upheaval, felt unbearable. Instead, I turned away from the view, focusing instead on the intricate pattern of the rug, trying desperately to force my racing heart to slow its frantic rhythm.
